intro:
Ahab (Hebrew Ach'av, "father's brother") was the son of Omri and the seventh king of the northern kingdom of Israel, reigning about 874–853 BC. Though politically able and militarily strong, he is remembered in Scripture as the most wicked of Israel's kings, the great patron of Baal worship, and the chief antagonist of the prophet Elijah — a king whose reign marks the spiritual nadir of the divided monarchy.

The historian's verdict is severe: "Ahab the son of Omri did evil in the sight of the LORD, more than all who were before him" (1 Kings 16:30). The decisive turn was his marriage to Jezebel, daughter of the Sidonian king Ethbaal, who imported the cult of Baal and Asherah as state religion, slaughtered the prophets of the LORD, and dominated her husband's will (1 Kings 16:31–33). Ahab built a temple and altar to Baal in Samaria, so that it was said he did more to provoke the LORD than all the kings of Israel before him.

Ahab's reign is shaped by his collision with Elijah. The prophet announced a drought of three and a half years as judgment (1 Kings 17:1), and at its climax called Ahab and the nation to Mount Carmel, where fire fell from heaven, the people confessed "The LORD, he is God," and the prophets of Baal were destroyed (1 Kings 18:38–39). Despite this, Ahab did not turn. The depth of his guilt is exposed in the affair of Naboth's vineyard: when Naboth refused to sell his ancestral land, Jezebel had him falsely accused and stoned so Ahab could seize it, and Elijah met the king with the terrible word that dogs would lick his blood where they had licked Naboth's, and would devour Jezebel (1 Kings 21:19; 1 Kings 21:23).

Yet the narrative is not without a glimpse of mercy. When Ahab heard the sentence he tore his clothes, fasted, and "went about dejectedly," and the LORD, noting his humbling, deferred the disaster to his son's days (1 Kings 21:27–29) — a striking testimony to God's readiness to relent even toward the worst of sinners who humble themselves. Ahab's end came as prophesied: ignoring the true prophet Micaiah and listening to flattering court prophets, he went to war against Aram at Ramoth-gilead, where a man drew his bow "at random" and struck the disguised king between the joints of his armor; he died, and the dogs licked up his blood (1 Kings 22:34–38). Ahab stands ever after as the Bible's paradigm of a king ruined by idolatry and a weak conscience yoked to a ruthless will.
